title: Stop Fixing Your Claude Skills. Autoresearch Does It For You
summary: Nick Saraev demonstrates how to automatically improve Claude Code skills by combining Karpathy's autoresearch principles with evals. The approach provides a step-by-step walkthrough for practitioners to optimize their own LLM-based skills through automated evaluation and refinement.
writeup: This video demonstrates how to apply Andrej Karpathy's auto-research methodology to Claude Code skills, turning a manually authored skill prompt into a self-improving system driven by automated evals. The presenter walks through a diagram-generator skill as the working example, showing how an agent iterates on the skill's markdown file every few minutes, scores each run against a binary eval suite, and retains only improvements. The core argument: skills are noisy distributions, not deterministic programs, so reliability requires repeated measurement against a fixed standard rather than one-off testing.

similar.title: How Claude Code's Creator ACTUALLY Automates his work

key_points: Karpathy's auto-research repo uses a three-file pattern (prepared.py, train.py, program.md) that maps directly onto Claude Code: skill.md plays the role of train.py (the thing being optimized) and a program.md agent wrapper plays the role of the autonomous optimizer.

Auto-research requires three ingredients: an objective numeric metric (called an eval or evaluation pass rate for skills), an automated measurement tool with no human in the loop, and a mutable artifact to change (the skill's prompt markdown).

The presenter applied the same methodology to a legacy website and reduced load time from roughly 1,100 milliseconds to 67 milliseconds across 67 test iterations, an 81.3% improvement, demonstrating the technique generalizes beyond skills.

Evals for the diagram-generator skill use four binary yes/no criteria: text legibility and grammatical correctness, adherence to a pastel color palette, linear left-to-right or top-to-bottom layout, and absence of numbers or ordinals in the diagram.

Because LLM outputs are distributions rather than fixed values, a valid eval must run the skill many times and assess the mode and median of results, not a single sample, before accepting a prompt revision as an improvement.

The accumulated list of attempted changes produced by auto-research runs is itself a durable asset: it can be handed to a future, more capable model (e.g., GPT-6 or Opus 5) to continue optimization from where predecessors stopped.
